danger_signals.visual_form: Sprite animations of goblins, trolls, invisible shades, flying lizards, will-o'-the-wisps, flesh-eating slimes, giant worms, spider, water norfalc
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate combat threat; player must engage in active dodge/sword mechanics or use fireball spells to dispatch

danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental hazards scattered throughout labyrinth levels; exact visual representation not documented
danger_signals.meaning: Instant death risk if triggered unwisely; requires puzzle-solving and cautious navigation

danger_signals.visual_form: Magical items and objects found in chests or looted from bodies; certain items cause instant death if used incorrectly
danger_signals.meaning: Knowledge/skill gate; player must understand item purpose before use or face death penalty

danger_signals.visual_form: Touching an NPC character triggers transition to 'combat mode' with different mechanics
danger_signals.meaning: Combat encounter locked; dodge/swing/stab actions required; higher mechanical pressure than field interaction

safety_signals.meaning: Rest/respawn points; transitions to dream sequences; narrative/mechanical safe zones

safety_signals.visual_form: Mysterious merchant character in certain levels offering potions and magical items
safety_signals.meaning: Resource acquisition point; identified as helpful NPC (later revealed as Mordamir in disguise)

safety_signals.visual_form: Goblin faction turns from hostile to allied after their king is spared
safety_signals.meaning: Player-initiated faction shift through mercy mechanic; goblins provide protection/support

safety_signals.visual_form: Warrior NPC accompanying player as loyal bodyguard
safety_signals.meaning: Allied companion; assists in combat; signals progression through narrative (absent in later betrayal sequence)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Initially hostile; visual differentiation as 'king' vs standard goblin troops
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Occupy specific levels of labyrinth; engage in war with trolls; respond to mercy/sparing actions

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Hostile creatures engaged in active conflict with goblins
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: War with goblins; presence indicates contested territory within labyrinth

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Peaceful in ancient past; final dragon appears as end-game boss confrontation
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Caverns beneath dungeon; dragon water control systems; narrative context via dream sequences on straw beds

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Walled city; fountains of youth symbolism
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Revealed through dream visions; city destroyed by dragon retaliation; represents civilizational collapse context

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Both faction types present and engaged in combat across labyrinth levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Creatures attack player on sight; factions target each other; player can influence allegiance through sparing goblin king

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Goblin sprites change from attacking to passive/friendly stance; may differentiate through animation or positioning
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Goblins cease hostility; provide support; previously hostile areas become traversable

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Betrayal sequence: Mordamir's true nature revealed
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Merchant character exposed as Mordamir; Ulindor presence removed; replaced with goblin messenger sent by Mordamir
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs shift to hostile; dialogue reveals kidnapping plot; final confrontation boss appears

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Straw bed triggers narrative flashback sequences showing ancient dragon civilization and Erinoch city
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player enters passive vision mode; receives lore exposition; no immediate danger; mechanical pause from active gameplay

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Labyrinth structured across 7-8 levels (NES: 7; other versions: 8); visual complexity/danger escalates with depth
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy types vary by level; puzzle difficulty increases; access to flying carpet, levitation spell, barrel-paddling gated by level progression

environmental_storytelling.example: Player witnesses fall of dragon civilization and siege of Erinoch through sleep sequences; establishes that Mordamir murdered dragons 1,000 years ago; reveals kidnapping motivation and final enemy identity
Ulindor serves as initial trusted companion; Dunric reveals Mordamir's betrayal at labyrinth bottom; Dunric's daughter kidnapping becomes narrative lynchpin
Sparing goblin king changes faction state; demonstrates player agency affects world; puzzle solutions and item acquisition tied to understanding narrative context
Deathtraps scattered throughout labyrinth signal danger escalation; visual placement communicates progression difficulty; unsafe spaces require learned caution

Merchant appears helpful across multiple levels; visual indistinguishability from other NPCs; betrayal reveal recontextualizes all prior interactions; narrative subversion through visual/behavioral continuity

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Attack on sight; engaged in active war with trolls; wander/protect map portions; can be dispatched with fireball spells at range or sword/dodge in combat mode
Cease hostility; may provide passage or support; behavioral shift indicates successful faction diplomacy; presence signals safe territory
Continuous aggression; engaged in conflict with goblins; represent persistent environmental threat; no alliance option documented
Encounter type not fully detailed; presence signals hidden danger; suggests detection/avoidance mechanics required
Accompanies player; assists in combat; provides narrative continuity; signals safe allied presence
Removed from player presence; replaced by goblin messenger revealing Mordamir's orders; signals narrative shift to isolation/betrayal
Offers potions and magical items for gold; appears helpful; maintains disguise; behavioral consistency hides identity
Transitions to boss enemy; engages in end-game combat; confirms 1,000-year-old antagonist identity; represents civilizational legacy threat
Provides exposition at labyrinth bottom; reveals kidnapping plot; narrates dragon lore; triggers final narrative revelation before boss confrontation
Manifestation of Mordamir; retaliation agent against Erinoch's destruction; represents 1,000-year consequence chain; sole dragon survivor; requires end-game combat mechanics

notes: The Immortal uses isometric perspective to establish depth cues for environmental danger. Visual communication relies heavily on NPC presence/absence to signal narrative state (Ulindor removal signals betrayal escalation). Dream sequences on straw beds provide diegetic lore exposition without breaking gameplay flow. Faction communication operates through sprite differentiation and behavioral state change rather than color/sigil systems (goblins transition from attack animations to peaceful stance). The game's 'graphic violence' noted in sources suggests high-consequence enemy death animations reinforce danger signaling. Merchant disguise mechanic subverts visual trust—player must infer identity through narrative revelation rather than visual tells. Goblin king sparing mechanic demonstrates moral choice feedback through faction state change, creating emergent world-state communication. No exact color palettes or sprite dimensions provided in sources; analysis based on gameplay mechanics and narrative structure.
sources_used: tcrf - The Immortal (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor
